title: Cranberry sauce
subtitle: Dried cranberries simmered with orange and sugar into a soft sauce.
excerpt: Dried cranberries simmered with orange juice, orange zest, and caster sugar until the cranberries have softened and the sauce has thickened. Tart, fruity, and ready in fifteen minutes. Makes enough for a large Christmas table.
sourceNotes: Cranberry sauce is an American tradition that arrived on British Christmas tables through the twentieth-century influence of American food culture. Fresh cranberries are not widely grown in Britain and are seasonally difficult to source; dried cranberries, available year-round, produce a good sauce with a slightly more concentrated flavour than fresh. The combination of cranberry with orange is standard in both British and American versions.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a small container for up to three months. Thaw in the fridge overnight.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Keeps in the fridge for two weeks.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in the fridge for two weeks. Flavour improves after a day.

content.text: Dried cranberries need more liquid than fresh ones to soften: they begin as dehydrated fruit and must be rehydrated during cooking. The combination of orange juice and water gives the sauce its flavour and enough liquid to let the cranberries soften fully.
The sauce thickens as it cools. If it looks too runny in the pan, it will set to the right consistency at room temperature.
A small saucepan. No other equipment needed.

Put {{dried-cranberries}} of dried cranberries, the orange juice and zest, {{water}} of water, {{caster-sugar}} of sugar, and the cinnamon stick if using into a small saucepan. Bring to a simmer over a medium heat, stirring to dissolve the sugar. Cook for 12–15 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the cranberries are soft and the liquid has reduced to a syrupy consistency.
Remove the cinnamon stick. Taste and adjust the sugar. The sauce will thicken further as it cools. Serve at room temperature or chilled. Keeps in a jar in the fridge for two weeks.

items.symptom: Sauce is too thick when cold
items.cause: Reduced more than needed, or dried cranberries absorbed more liquid.
items.fix: Stir in a tablespoon of orange juice or water and warm briefly. Dried cranberries are starchier than fresh, so the sauce sets more firmly.

items.symptom: Cranberries are still chewy after 15 minutes
items.cause: Dried cranberries need time to rehydrate.
items.fix: Add another 50 ml of water and continue simmering. Harder dried cranberries take longer than fresh soft ones.

items.cause: Dried cranberries are sweeter than fresh.
items.fix: Add a squeeze of lemon juice to sharpen. Reduce the sugar by 20 g next time.
Add more sugar a teaspoon at a time until balanced.

content.text: Cranberry sauce entered the British Christmas table through American influence, accelerated by the expansion of American-style turkey traditions in the twentieth century. Turkey was a Christmas centrepiece in Britain long before cranberry sauce arrived with it: roast turkey with cranberry is an American combination that transferred to Britain as a package. The dried cranberry version here is a practical adaptation for a country where fresh cranberries are a seasonal import rather than a domestic crop.
